metadata.title: A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework

abstract: The recently forwarded Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework (PSMEF) allows researchers to study social media in terms of generalized types of user interfaces. This study formally extended the PSMEF via the Digital User Interface Model and replicated previous work by evidencing the existence of new (e.g., Overtly Algorithmic Content Pages) and validating previously identified types of user interfaces (e.g., Home Pages and Chats/Messages) that make up individuals' personal social media environments. Using topic modeling (i.e., Latent Dirichlet Allocation) and a novel mixed methods approach (i.e., schematic semantic network analysis), we quantitatively evidenced four distinct classes of user interfaces based on open-ended descriptions that participants provided for six popular social media platforms (i.e., Instagram, Snapchat, Facebook, Twitter, TikTok, and YouTube). Results inform on the qualitative differences between distinct user interface classes that underwrite users' experiences over social media, with implications for conceptualization and operationalization related to social media use.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: The question of for whom, under what conditions, and by what means social media comes to impact focal outcomes of interest, like mental health, has remained difficult to fully address in academic research. In part, this is due to the rapid maturation and diversification of social media platforms as a type of communication technology (Bayer et al., 2020;boyd, 2015). In response to changes seen across the commercial social media landscape, researchers have broadened their methodological approaches and theorizing to accommodate the increasingly dynamic, multiplatform social media reality experienced by users to assess social media use correlates. This includes transitions from single to multiplatform studies (e.g., Beyens et al., 2020), in addition to an emphasis on studying more contextually transcendent constructs (e.g., affordances) over platform features within the context of social mediaparticularly, as individual features differ widely across platforms as distinct technologies change over time (Ellison & Boyd, 2013;Evans et al., 2017;Bayer et al., 2020).

In line with these efforts to increase the generalizability of evidenced effects, recently introduced conceptualizations of social media have helped to advance novel ways of approaching the study of social media design and their corresponding effects. This includes theoretical frameworks, like the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework (PSMEF; Carter et al., 2023) and the concept of social media elements (Bayer et al., 2020), 1 both of which outline innovative ways to conceptualize, and thus operationally define, social media constructs to increase the generalizability of evidenced social media effects over time. For example, the PSMEF proposes that evidencing effects across certain user interface classes (UICs)-that is, different instantiations of interfaces with similar underlying features and affordances-can help to increase the generalizability of evidenced effects (Carter et al., 2023). This is because mapping effects in this way will facilitate the computation of aggregated and comparative effect sizes by helping to pair effects across the various environmental contexts over social media wherein people navigate (e.g., Home Tab on Instagram, Newsfeed on Facebook) and engage over time (e.g., Home Pages; Bayer et al., 2020;Carter et al., 2023). This can help to advance theory, as researchers can better understand which types of social media use or engagement are associated with various outcomes of interest, rather than operationalizing social media use more broadly, based on social media in a colloquial sense of the term, or by different use dimensions (Kaye, 2021;Valkenburg et al., 2022b).

In presenting the PSMEF, Carter et al. (2023) evidenced that adolescents psychologically associate certain classes of user interfaces (i.e., Profiles, Posts, Chats/Messages, Stories, and Location Pages) differentially with particular popular platforms (i.e., Instagram, Snapchat, Facebook, and Twitter).

This study also identified several salient types of user interface classes that functionally characterize adolescents' social media environments, including Profiles, Posts, Chats/Messages, Stories, Location Pages, Search Pages, Group Pages, Home Pages, and Comments/Comment Sections (see "The PSMEF" section below for definitions).

While informative in helping to identify generalized technological spaces that underpin users' experiences over social media, open questions remain. Foremost, Carter et al. (2023) thematically coded transcripts to identify distinct UICs, which required the specification of a priori categories (Carley, 1993;Doerfel, 1998). While helpful in establishing a preliminary basis for theorizing, the use of a pre-defined set of UIC categories inherently limited evidencing the validity of UICs themselves. Second, despite introducing different UICs, Carter et al. (2023) did not outline a way to categorize interfaces quantitatively-a needed step to validate interface class categories. Lastly, the PSMEF defines social media in terms of an interconnected set of navigable online locations (i.e., digital user interfaces), but did not integrate digital user interfaces as a construct with existing work on spatial mental representations. As the boundaries of different places remain psychologically defined (Tversky, 1993), integrating work on spatial mental representations with the concept of digital user interfaces would extend the PSMEF's theoretical utility.

Therefore, this study had three primary objectives. First, we sought to extend previous work on the PSMEF by inductively exploring the types of user interface classes that exist over six popular social media brands: Snapchat, Facebook, Twitter, Instagram, YouTube, and TikTok. Second, we sought to also introduce a new computational approach to study users' social media environments-particularly, a structured form of semantic network analysis, schematic semantic network analysis (SSNA). The introduction of SSNA extends initial work on the PSMEF (Carter et al., 2023) by providing a means to model interface groupings quantitatively based on a shared understanding of their composition among a given sample population. Third, we provide a formal extension to the PSMEF, the Digital User Interface Model, by basing the concept of digital user interfaces on the contemporaneous properties of spatial mental representations (Tversky, 1993). The extension more clearly illustrates digital user interfaces conceptually, formally outlines their properties as flexible mental representations, and provides a way to think about how they generally interrelate with other PSMEF constructs (i.e., back-end systems; Carter et al., 2023).

To do so, we first review the PSMEF and introduce the Digital User Interface Model. After, we present a study to apply these frameworks. Specifically, we quantitatively analyzed open-ended interface descriptions from a sample of college students (N ¼ 380; ages 18-34) to identify distinct UICs over six popular social media platforms using two complementary methods: SSNA and a popular form of text analysis (i.e., Latent Dirichlet Allocation [LDA]). Results inform on the composition of distinct UICs based on the descriptive words participants used (e.g., posts, picture, family, search, and peers) to describe different online spaces they typically navigate to over several prominent platforms. In doing so, we extended and replicated previous work on the PSMEF conceptually, methodologically, and demographically (e.g., exhibit that previous findings generalize to a sample of young adults).

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: Because platforms represent pluralistic and dynamic constructs, they cannot serve as the sole basis to study social media effects over time (Bayer et al., 2020;Carter et al., 2023). One solution to this issue of building out a systematic basis for social media effects research resides with leveraging design patterns as a basis for mapping effects to particular types of design spaces that exist within and across platforms (Bayer et al., 2020;Carter et al., 2023). The PSMEF can serve as a theoretical framework for research in this area (Carter et al., 2023).

The PSMEF allows researchers to study social media in a way that capitalizes on the diversity of social media systems. To do so, the framework conceptualizes social media in terms of users' personal social media environments (PSMEs), using constructs derived from Ecological Systems Theory (EST), a prominent theory of environmental systems in the context of human development (Bronfenbrenner, 2005). Following EST's conceptualization of environmental systems as an individual-centric set of nested environmental structures, the PSMEF explicates concepts like digital user interface, digital microsystem, digital mesosystem, digital exosystem, and UICs (Carter et al., 2023). As a result, the PSMEF both mirrors and extends EST's initial formulation of its nested human ecology through its adaptation of constructs like an offline setting, microsystem, mesosystem, and exosystem to interactive media environments.

According to the PSMEF, social media is an expansive digital landscape made up of the various mediated spaces that serve to underwrite users' experiences, centered around those spaces that facilitate social interaction (Carter et al., 2023). Often this is conceptualized in terms of channels of communication, platforms, features, or user interfaces (e.g., chat room, Facebook profile; Carr & Hayes, 2015;Meier & Reinecke, 2021). However, to provide a basis for theorizing about social media as an environmental context, the PSMEF conceptualized distinct instantiations of particular user interfaces over a live platform as an environmental unit (i.e., digital user interfaces). Thus, while user interfaces (e.g., Instagram's Profile Interface) operate as a template for certain segments within popular apps, digital user interfaces represent realizations of those interfaces wherein users can navigate (e.g., a given Instagram profile). Such digital user interfaces remain analogous to the idea of an offline location, but over mediated spaces (Carter et al., 2023)-essentially, different in-platform sections defined for some purpose and understood to reflect a distinct space (Uddin & Gutwin, 2021). A more technical definition could include a view or sub-view (e.g., screen segment) that is rendered from a coded function or class object.foot_0 Digital user interfaces in the context of the PSMEF, thus, function as the basis of social media as a contemporary digital landscape.

Digital user interfaces can then come together to form a more superordinate sprawl of mediated environments (e.g., the Internet, a platform), with each representing a seemingly unique space from the standpoint of each user. To account for this, the PSMEF proposed that for any given user, each digital user interface reflects a digital microsystem, a digital location characterized by the unique roles, norms, and perceived audiences contextualizing that space for them (Carter et al., 2023).

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: The PSMEF's conceptualization of constructs like digital user interfaces and digital microsystems provides a theoretical basis for defining users' PSMEs. Particularly, by integrating these concepts, the PSMEF defines PSMEs as a user-centric digital environment, centered around a subset of digital microsystems that facilitate social interaction, embedded in one or more live platforms, broader applications (e.g., data pipelines), device(s), and an individual's offline social contexts (Carter et al., 2023). As a result, the PSMEF, in making linkages between interactive media and EST, provides a theoretical foundation for the study of social media as a digital ecology in extension to EST (Bronfenbrenner, 2005).

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
sections.header_raw: Social media elements and user interface classes
sections.header_norm: social media elements and user interface classes
paragraphs: Lastly, the PSMEF affords new ways to conceptualize social media in terms of more generalized technological structures, and thus, social media as a dynamic environmental context. The primary way it accomplishes this is by directly mapping the concept of social media elements to users' PSMEs (Carter et al., 2023). Theoretically, all digital user interfaces (e.g., user A's profile on Facebook) and digital microsystems (e.g., user A's profile on Facebook from the standpoint of a particular observer) represent a derivative of a UIC (e.g., Profiles as a general type of user interface). UICs operate as the primary conceptual basis for the study of social media use correlates over time in the PSMEF (Carter et al., 2023), and itself stems from the concept of social media elements (Bayer et al., 2020).

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
sections.header_raw: User interface classes and PSMEs: initial evidence
sections.header_norm: user interface classes and psmes initial evidence
paragraphs: Initial research has outlined the types of UICs that characterize social media users' PSMEs (Bayer et al., 2020;Carter et al., 2023). By thematically coding types of user interfaces represented across statements adolescents made about social media via a set of eight qualitative focus groups among adolescent (ages 13-17) participants, Carter et al. (2023) identified several additional types of UICs, including Stories, Posts, Comment Sections/Comments, Location Pages, Search Pages, Home Pages, and Group Pages. Together, these UICs can be used by researchers to better conceptualize social media across platforms.

Investigating social media at different levels of abstraction While social media is often operationalized at different levels of abstraction across social media effects studies (Meier & Reinecke, 2021), the PSMEF would advocate for operationalizing social media constructs at the level of specific interfaces. The rationale for this is twofold. On one hand, the PSMEF's focus on interfaces is because of the benefit of extrapolating evidenced effects over time due to the iterative, persistent, and platform agnostic nature of common types of interface designs (see Bayer et al., 2020;Carter et al., 2023). On the other, it is because interfaces, like platforms, represent bounded units of code (cf. Carter et al., 2023) and an objective location.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
sections.header_raw: Digital user interfaces as hierarchical, spatial representations
sections.header_norm: digital user interfaces as hierarchical spatial representations
paragraphs: While the PSMEF organized PSMEs, social media elements, and other social media constructs based on their level of abstraction (Carter et al., 2023), it did not extend this same logic to digital user interfaces in particular. Spatial representations, as psychological constructs, vary in their level of abstraction and scope, activating in a cognitively online fashion (i.e., at a given point in time) based on the co-activation of psychologically relevant situational cues (Tversky, 1993). As a mental representation, digital user interfaces inherently take on a flexible, but hierarchical structure, and integrating this logic into the PSMEF would aid in interface-based theorizing. Doing so would help to better inform how different interface constructs coincide with other PSMEF constructs, like a platform's underlying back-end systems (i.e., application), and further explain why certain PSMEF constructs differ in their level of abstraction (e.g., why platforms are more abstract than interfaces) as a function of how interfaces are conceptualized as a type of digital space.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: The model presents interfaces across a range of abstraction because digital user interfaces fundamentally represent a discrete location as understood by an individual user, which may vary in scope as a function of how a user psychologically construes a digital user interface at a given point in time. As people come to understand user interfaces in terms of system objects, their relationships, and potential object operations (i.e., conceptual model; Foley & Van Dam, 1982), interface cues should associate with certain mental heuristics and behavioral representations (e.g., Sundar et al., 2015). In this model (Figure 1), a platform and its underlying back-end components (i.e., application) are represented as a triangle, extended from a digital user interface as a type of spatial mental representation-a psychological reference frame for a given interface construct; the spheres in the illustration represent lower order situation-relevant cues, like environmental features and behavior. The farther right the dashed line across the x-axis of the triangle, the more abstract the conceptualization/operationalization of the interface, with the construct encompassing a wider scope of back-end application components (e.g., data flows, algorithmic systems, etc.) over a platform (see Box A). The scope of the application encompassed by a given interface construct is illustrated on the left side of the dashed line. Interface representations within the box (see Box C) represent examples of interfaces operationalized/conceptualized in line with the PSMEF's conception of digital user interfaces as a technological construct. 3 A platform as an interface is illustrated on the far right and encapsulates an entire platform as a singular, but bounded application.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
sections.header_raw: Leveraging the Digital User Interface Model to quantitatively model user interfaces
sections.header_norm: leveraging the digital user interface model to quantitatively model user interfaces
paragraphs: The Digital User Interface Model outlines that one way to quantitatively evidence the validity of certain UIC categories would be to model users' understanding of the interfaces they use across their PSMEs. According to the Digital User Interface Model, interfaces as mental representations inherently represent a set of schema and sub-schema-psychological structures detailing objects (e.g., platform features, actions) and how they interrelate (Shehata et al., 2021). This makes investigating schema across groups of people a seemingly ideal basis to explore UIC categories. Since a person's conception of a digital user interface provides a partial, rather than comprehensive account of environmental cues composing a particular place (i.e., an interface), the Digital User Interface Model would advocate that interfaces remain best modeled as group-level representations; they afford a more accurate model of the world as it objectively exists (Woelfel & Fink, 1980;Rauthmann & Sherman, 2020). How interface concepts map onto particular social media features and other related concepts as a function of individuals' schematic understanding of their PSME is less clear, however. Nevertheless, the types of UICs identified, in addition to their features, should remain consistent with those evidenced by previous work using samples of both adolescents and young adults (e.g., Bayer et al., 2020;Carter et al., 2023). Therefore, we propose and ask the following: Notes. Above, we outline a theoretical extension to the PSMEF's initial conceptualization of digital user interfaces as discrete, mediated locations (i.e., Box B) and how interfaces as a construct map onto application systems within the PSMEF (Box C). Example "regex" filters are provided below example PSMEF digital user interface, as technological constructs, to illustrate ways to operationalize each based on URL patterns.

exchange for their participation in the online survey. Seventy-one percent of participants identified as female (n ¼ 270), 25% male (n ¼ 95), 1% non-binary (n ¼ 5), and the remaining other/prefer not to answer (n ¼ 10). Seventyfive percent identified as heterosexual (n ¼ 285), 8% bisexual (n ¼ 31), 5% asexual (n ¼ 18), and 2% lesbian/gay (n ¼ 9); 10% fell outside of these categories or preferred not to answer (n ¼ 22). Participants ranged from ages 18 to 34 (M ¼ 19.41, SD ¼ 2.01). The majority identified as Asian (57%, n ¼ 215), followed by White (22%, n ¼ 85), Hispanic/Latino (17%, n ¼ 64), Black (5%, n ¼ 19), and American Indian or Alaska Native (4%, n ¼ 16).

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: Participants were recruited via an online sample pool of college students from a public university located in California between November 2020 and February 2021. Subjects were asked to report on their use of social media. This included three primary measures (see below). Participants concluded their participation by completing a set of demographic questions and were thanked for their time. The project was approved by the Institutional Review Board at the University of California, Davis. 4 Sample size considerations were determined based on a separate study completed in the same online survey. Thus, there was no a priori sample size justification used for this study.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: Participants were asked to report on the social media platforms they use, spanning a set of popular social media platforms (i.e., Instagram, Facebook, Twitter, Snapchat, YouTube, TikTok, Reddit, Tumblr, WeChat, Messenger, and WhatsApp), in addition to others not listed. Usage rates are listed by the percentage of participants who reported using an app out of the final 380 participants: Instagram (92%), YouTube (84%), Snapchat (69%), TikTok (67%), Facebook (53%), Twitter (48%), Messenger (39%), Reddit (25%), WhatsApp (24%), WeChat (20%), Tumblr (6%), and other (3%).

Pages/sections used by platform Lastly, participants completed an open-ended question asking them to detail the social media pages/sections they typically navigate to over each social media app they reported using. For each, they were also prompted to detail, in a paragraph, its attributes and features, who they encounter while in the space (e.g., strangers, celebrities, acquaintances, friends, and family), and what generally occurs on the platform while they are using it. sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: To investigate H1 and RQ1, we quantitatively analyzed the screened open-ended interface descriptions to identify distinct UICs over six popular social media platforms using two complementary methods: SSNA (described below) and a popular form of text analysis (i.e., LDA). After, we probed user interface classes' compositions as a function of how interfaces differentially associated with the semantic features (e.g., posts, picture, family, search, and peers) participants used to describe them.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: Each entry provided by participants and its corresponding section description were screened and subsequently coded for the top used platforms in the sample: Instagram (N ¼ 474), Facebook (N ¼ 162), Snapchat (N ¼ 286), Twitter (N ¼ 154), TikTok (N ¼ 248), and YouTube (N ¼ 287). We used reference frames as offered by subjects to demarcate different pages/sections comprising each platform, which spanned digital user interfaces (e.g., particular Facebook group page), primary pages (e.g., home page) listed within platforms' navigation menus (e.g., navigation bar), content streams (e.g., my feed on Instagram, For You Page on TikTok, and Trending on Twitter), and individual content structures (e.g., videos). While each could operate as a unit of analysis to derive thematic categories for distinct feature classes (e.g., user interfaces and content streams), user interfaces partially networked into fixed aspects of platform systems' navigational structure, like a navigation bar or menu, providing a seemingly ideal point of reference from which to derive distinct UICs. This is because menus operate as a primary organizing structure across platforms (Shneiderman et al., 2017).

The first author and another co-author reviewed each page/ section description to derive a set of thematic categories reflective of prominent user interfaces over each platform for coding. User interfaces tended to map onto most of the primary navigation icons listed across each platform's navigational bar (e.g., Direct Messages on Instagram, Home on TikTok). For a listing of each user interface category by platform, see Table 2. A subset of page/section entries (approx. 20%) were selected at random to assess intercoder reliability for each platform across the identified user interface categories. Krippendorff's alpha indicated a high degree of intercoder reliability across each platform: Instagram (a ¼ :958), Snapchat (a ¼ :974), TikTok (a ¼ :967), YouTube (a ¼ :975), Twitter (a ¼ :949), and Facebook (a ¼ :917). Remaining entries were split and coded independently. For frequency distributions across individual user interfaces, see Supplementary Figure S1 (Supplementary Materials). Categorizations produced for the reliability set were resolved for analysis by randomly selecting coded values between the two coders.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: As results can vary by methods and data processing decisions, it is important to validate findings using multiple approaches (Grimmer et al., 2022). To do so, we employed two complementary forms of text analysis to demonstrate the robustness of our results: LDA and SSNA. Taken together, LDA provides a means to evidence initial UICs represented across participant descriptions using probabilistic modeling techniques. In addition, SSNA provides a means to explore, in more nuanced detail, UICs and their corresponding attributes via network analysis. This approach directly complements the LDA model, as it provides measures regarding how interface concepts contribute to the underlying dimensionality of the larger representation, or network (i.e., descriptions as a whole), via localized network metrics (e.g., centrality). Lastly, the dual modeling approach enables model validation across methods.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: LDA, as a popular method to extract latent structures (i.e., topics) from text data (Grimmer et al., 2022), inductively models distributions of topics across units of text (e.g., descriptions) and can simultaneously model topics as a probabilistic distribution of words (Blei, 2012). Topics are selected based on a multinomial language model estimated by word frequencies across text units as a function of word co-occurrences (Grimmer et al., 2022); words with high levels of co-occurrence across user interface descriptions should more likely map onto the same topic. LDA, thus, provides a means to evidence UICs and their key attributes by modeling latent structures from open-ended descriptions.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: In addition, we introduce SSNA as a mixed methods approach to both classify and probe user interfaces, in addition to UIC attributes. Based on the Galileo Method, which takes a matrix of pairwise similarity ratings between a set of concepts to compute concept similarity (Woelfel & Fink, 1980), SSNA uses semantic features derived from text descriptions as a general psychological subspace for the target population to model similarities between mental constructs (e.g., a person's conception of their Home Tab on Instagram). This takes the form of an adjacency matrix, c Â f, wherein a primary concept, c i , is defined by a vector, c i ! , derived from semantic features present across its corresponding descriptions. Each feature represents a single dimension of a broader mental subspace that is reflective of a schematic manifold, R f , and can classify primary concepts as more or less similar as a function of their associative relationships among some set of semantic features, f i...n . In this way, SSNA is consistent with cognitive schema theory such that it defines mental constructs hierarchically as a function of their association with subordinate concepts (Shehata et al., 2021). As a user interface is represented as a set of semantic vectors (i.e., c i ! ) across R f , a UIC, UIC i , can be defined as a set of user interfaces (UI), UI ij , with more intragroup connections to a set of semantic features (i.e., edges), f i...n , than some other subset of UI.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: The number of topics (k) for LDA is specified a priori and can lead to more or less meaningful representations at varying levels of k. We used topic coherence-a measure of a topic's semantic interpretability (Srinivasa-Desikan, 2018)-to determine k for our final model; higher topic coherence values equate to more meaningful topics. Descriptions were preprocessed (i.e., removed stop words and punctuation, lowercased entries) and lemmatized given the size of our corpus (Grimmer et al., 2022) postjNOUN) using spaCy to further contextualize UIC attributes. To increase the accuracy of the SSNA representation, we dropped semantic features with frequencies below the mean. This allowed us to model predominant semantic structures across our sampled population, one more reflective of shared meaning structures (e.g., culturally endowed concepts); the resulting graph provides a group-level representation (Woelfel & Fink, 1980). This yielded a set of 286 concepts for the first SSNA network. In our second network, we used the topics generated from our LDA model to inform on which interfaces to aggregate and further probe the semantic attributes of our identified UICs (n ¼ 245 concepts). Interfaces falling within each of the clustered groupings yielded by our first SSNA network were collapsed into a single node in our second SSNA network. The latter SSNA network allowed for the exploration of UIC attributes. Interfaces categorized as "other" were dropped from the visualization and analysis of both SSNA networks. Network analysis methods, like clustering and community detection, can also be applied in SSNA to identify groups of primary concepts, including UICs and their attributes, based on the primary concepts' degree of intra-group connections with particular semantic features. To do so, we leveraged modularity analysis using the Louvain method, a community detection algorithm; nodes that cluster together maintain more intragroup associations than nodes external to the clustered group (Blondel et al., 2008).

Multiple indicators offer information about a node's position in the network. Centrality denotes the importance, prominence, or power of a concept in a network or how central a concept is within a body of text (Barnett et al., 2010). In line with graph theory, various centrality measures function to calculate the importance of any given network node (e.g., primary semantic concept) based on its connections to other nodes (e.g., descriptive semantic features). For example, the simplest form of centrality, degree centrality, indexes the number of links for each node. We used eigenvector centrality as it is a generalized form of degree that includes a node's extended connections (i.e., edges of the nodes its connected with) to calculate node prominence (Bonacich, 1987). Prominent user interfaces falling with certain modalities or groupings, indexed by higher eigenvector centrality values, should denote a potential type of UIC that functionally demarcates users' understanding of social media based on their PSMEs. This is because eigenvector centrality can identify concepts with influence more globally over the whole network as opposed to only nodes directly linked to the focal node; it is a scaled value expressed as a percentage and is often used to identify important thematic concepts in text data (e.g., Ruiz & Barnett, 2015). Interfaces with high eigenvector centrality scores across each cluster should maintain the same qualitative attributes of groupings evidenced by the LDA model.

Network visualizations and metrics were produced using Gephi (Bastian et al., 2009). Nodes are colored by modularity class, with the same color among interfaces denoting stronger intragroup connections across the same-colored semantic features. Tie width is reflective of concept co-occurrence such that wider lines denote higher rates of co-occurrence across individual page/section descriptions.
sections.text:
The number of topics (k) for LDA is specified a priori and can lead to more or less meaningful representations at varying levels of k. We used topic coherence-a measure of a topic's semantic interpretability (Srinivasa-Desikan, 2018)-to determine k for our final model; higher topic coherence values equate to more meaningful topics. Descriptions were preprocessed (i.e., removed stop words and punctuation, lowercased entries) and lemmatized given the size of our corpus (Grimmer et al., 2022) postjNOUN) using spaCy to further contextualize UIC attributes. To increase the accuracy of the SSNA representation, we dropped semantic features with frequencies below the mean. This allowed us to model predominant semantic structures across our sampled population, one more reflective of shared meaning structures (e.g., culturally endowed concepts); the resulting graph provides a group-level representation (Woelfel & Fink, 1980). This yielded a set of 286 concepts for the first SSNA network. In our second network, we used the topics generated from our LDA model to inform on which interfaces to aggregate and further probe the semantic attributes of our identified UICs (n ¼ 245 concepts). Interfaces falling within each of the clustered groupings yielded by our first SSNA network were collapsed into a single node in our second SSNA network. The latter SSNA network allowed for the exploration of UIC attributes. Interfaces categorized as "other" were dropped from the visualization and analysis of both SSNA networks. Network analysis methods, like clustering and community detection, can also be applied in SSNA to identify groups of primary concepts, including UICs and their attributes, based on the primary concepts' degree of intra-group connections with particular semantic features. To do so, we leveraged modularity analysis using the Louvain method, a community detection algorithm; nodes that cluster together maintain more intragroup associations than nodes external to the clustered group (Blondel et al., 2008).

Multiple indicators offer information about a node's position in the network. Centrality denotes the importance, prominence, or power of a concept in a network or how central a concept is within a body of text (Barnett et al., 2010). In line with graph theory, various centrality measures function to calculate the importance of any given network node (e.g., primary semantic concept) based on its connections to other nodes (e.g., descriptive semantic features). For example, the simplest form of centrality, degree centrality, indexes the number of links for each node. We used eigenvector centrality as it is a generalized form of degree that includes a node's extended connections (i.e., edges of the nodes its connected with) to calculate node prominence (Bonacich, 1987). Prominent user interfaces falling with certain modalities or groupings, indexed by higher eigenvector centrality values, should denote a potential type of UIC that functionally demarcates users' understanding of social media based on their PSMEs. This is because eigenvector centrality can identify concepts with influence more globally over the whole network as opposed to only nodes directly linked to the focal node; it is a scaled value expressed as a percentage and is often used to identify important thematic concepts in text data (e.g., Ruiz & Barnett, 2015). Interfaces with high eigenvector centrality scores across each cluster should maintain the same qualitative attributes of groupings evidenced by the LDA model.

Network visualizations and metrics were produced using Gephi (Bastian et al., 2009). Nodes are colored by modularity class, with the same color among interfaces denoting stronger intragroup connections across the same-colored semantic features. Tie width is reflective of concept co-occurrence such that wider lines denote higher rates of co-occurrence across individual page/section descriptions.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: H1 predicted that interfaces across each of the six social media platforms would cluster together to represent previously identified UICs (e.g., Messages/Chats, Home Pages, etc.) and RQ1 asked if other types of UICs exist across Facebook, Instagram, Twitter, Snapchat, YouTube, and TikTok. To initially assess H1 and RQ1, we first ran several topic models to determine the number of topics most suitable for our data. Topic coherence scores peaked at three topics (see Supplementary Figure S2) and then subsequently diminished in value. Thus, three topics were used to estimate our LDA model. See Table 3 for top words by topic (also see Supplementary Figure S6). Topics 1 and 3 mapped onto Chats/Messages and Home Pages as previously identified UICs, supporting H1. Topic 2 appeared to map onto in-app pages indexing Out-of-Network Content Pages (e.g., recommended videos), suggesting the emergence of a distinct, previously unidentified UIC.

To further validate H1 and RQ1, we calculated modularity across our first SSNA network multiple times to assess the consistency of our modularity results. This yielded four primary clusters 90% of the time, indicating a consistent solution. For a listing of all user interfaces by their modality class, see Table 4.

Cluster 1 encompassed 30.42% of the semantic concepts across the network and mapped onto Topic 2. We refer to this grouping as Overtly Algorithmic Content Pages (OACP). 7 User interfaces in this cluster included Home on TikTok, Home on YouTube, Reels on Instagram, Shorts on YouTube, and Spotlight on Snapchat; each contains a tailored algorithmic content stream of videos. Cluster 2 spanned 23.43% of the semantic concepts across the network and mapped onto Topic 3 (i.e., Home Pages). This included Home on Instagram, Home on Twitter, and Home on Facebookinterfaces prototypical to home pages. Cluster 3 mapped most closely to Topic 2, and spanned 21.33% of the semantic concepts across the network. This cluster was representative of Search Pages as a UIC grouping; it included Explore on Instagram, Search on Twitter, Discover on TikTok, Search on YouTube, and Search on Facebook. Cluster 4 spanned 19.58% of the semantic concepts across the network, with interfaces representative of Chats/Messages characterizing it as a grouping (i.e., Topic 1). This cluster included Chat on Snapchat, Direct Messages on Instagram, Inbox on TikTok, Messenger on Facebook, and Messages on Twitter. The groupings of the aforementioned interfaces mapped onto the same groupings between 60% and 100% of the time across modularity iterations: Cluster 1 (100%), Cluster 2 (60%), Cluster 3 (80%), and Cluster 4 (100%). 8 In all, our SSNA analysis also supported H1 and evidenced four stable classes of user interfaces (RQ1).

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: Lastly, to explore the types of features/attributes that characterize prominent UICs among participants' PSMEs, we took two additional steps. First, our initial LDA model illustrated key attributes of the four identified UICs (see Table 3). To further validate and contextualize UIC attributes, we collapsed the identified, predominant groupings of UI concepts for each cluster (see bolded user interfaces in Table 4) into single nodes to investigate their common features as a type of UIC in a separate SSNA network based on our LDA topics. Stories on Snapchat were also added to the bolded concepts from Cluster 2 due to their representativeness as a type of Home Page and association with other Home Pages (e.g., classified in the same cluster as Home on Instagram and Facebook 40% of the time). The cluster analysis across the secondary SSNA network (see Supplementary Figure S7) resulted in four primary clusters of semantic features for each UIC concept. For a full listing of attributes by grouping, see Supplementary Table S1 (Supplementary Materials). Cluster 1 accounted for 26.55% of concepts and the UIC, OACPs. Semantic attributes for OACPs as UIC benchmarked interfaces containing a tailored algorithmic content stream that exposed users largely to out-of-network (e.g., stranger and influencer), yet curated video content. Cluster 2 accounted for 16.73% of concepts and the UIC, Search Pages. Semantic attributes for Search Pages as a UIC indexed that it encompassed UIs including a search bar and topically curated outof-network content streams which users use to access information (e.g., entertainment and fashion). Cluster 3 accounted for 16.33% of concepts and the UIC, Chats/ Messages. Interfaces characteristic of Chats/Messages indexed those facilitating private, yet directed forms of reciprocal social interaction (e.g., text, send, and respond). Cluster 4 accounted for 40% of concepts and the UIC, Home Pages. In all, Home Pages served as a set of interfaces containing in-network content streams (e.g., acquaintance, friend, and family) indexing recent events, which users generally construe as public.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: When investigating topic distributions among the semantic attributes grouped to particular UICs, 9 semantic features grouped to each class (i.e., secondary SSNA network, Supplementary Figure S7) contained percentages of words that corresponded highest with their corresponding latent topic. Ninety-one percent of the semantic attributes mapped to the Chats/Messages cluster in our second SSNA network corresponded to the Chats/Messages latent topic, 96% of OACPs to the Out-of-Network Content Pages latent topic, and 88% of Home Pages to the Home Page latent topic. For Search Pages, 85% of the clustered semantic attributes mapped onto Out-of-Network Content Pages latent topic. When collapsed across relevant UI concepts (see bolded interface concepts, Table 4) on a description-by-description basis, mean topic distributions corresponded highest with their respective latent topic. Seventy-four percent of descriptions of Chat/Message interfaces mapped onto the Chat/Messages latent topic, 66% of descriptions for Home Page interfaces mapped onto the Home Page latent topic, and 71% of OACPs mapped onto the Out-of-Network Content Pages latent topic. For Search Pages, topic distributions across descriptions of Search Page interfaces were highest for the Out-of-Network Content Pages latent topic at 56%. In all, our results were largely consistent across methods.

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: Social media represents a "moving target" for study due to technological (e.g., platform updates) and cultural shifts (e.g., divergent patterns or conventions of use) that often affect its general composition as a type of mediated environment (Ellison & boyd, 2013;Bayer et al., 2020). To account for this fluctuation, the PSMEF seeks to map social media effects to more generalized social media constructs that structurally compose the types of design spaces users engage within (e.g., Chats/Messages and Home Pages). In furthering the PSMEF's concept of digital user interfaces as spatial mental representations, we introduced the Digital User Interface Model, which outlines the contemporary properties of digital user interfaces as psychological constructs existing at various levels of abstraction. Leveraging this account, we employed a set of mixed, complementary analytical methods to quantitatively analyze a set of open-ended survey responses indexing the user interfaces (i.e., pages/sections) that a sample of young adults reported commonly using over six popular platforms (i.e., TikTok, Instagram, Snapchat, YouTube, Facebook, and Twitter) as group-level representations. In addition to evidencing the validity of previously identified types of UICs (Bayer et al., 2020;Carter et al., 2023), our data clearly exhibit the theoretical and methodological benefits of SSNA and LDA in the context of the PSMEF and the study of social media. Results suggest that social media use is largely consolidated across four general user interfaces classes that facilitate varying forms of social interaction, supporting the PSMEF.
A replication and extension of the PSMEF: six prominent platforms

Perhaps the most promising aspect of the PMSEF is its replicability and the underlying utility of its constructs, like UICs. This study sought to extend previous work on the PSMEF by modeling UICs across social media, introduce a new mixed methods approach to contribute to this aim (i.e., SSNA), and generalize preliminary UIC categories previously identified to a different sample population (i.e., young adults). In previous work among a sample of adolescents (Carter et al., 2023), references made about user interfaces were grouped into a set of thematically derived UIC categories as a function of user interfaces' similarity across certain features and affordances.

Taking a more inductive approach, this study (a) had participants report on the pages/sections they typically navigate to when using social media and (b) describe their understanding of each in detail. This is significant as each participant description served as a representation, based on each user's conception, of core spaces making up their PSME. We then classified each reported page/section to one of several primary user interfaces exhibited on each app's navigational menus (e.g., Home on Instagram, Search on Twitter, and Chats on Snapchat) and then (c) quantitatively modeled each interface using the semantic features participants used to describe them. In this way, through SSNA and LDA, we used the semantic features derived from text descriptions as a general mental model of social media to benchmark interface groupings and their attributes into a group-level representation. On one hand, as inductive analytical techniques, LDA and SSNA afforded us the capacity to assess interface similarity both quantitatively and qualitatively across platforms without any a priori constraints (e.g., thematic categories) and provided further evidence, among participants from a different population from that of Carter et al. (2023), for the validity of Chats/Messages, Home Pages, and Search Pages as previously identified UICs. For instance, each emerged as core UICs. Characteristic attributes of each UIC, in addition to patterns of social media use associated therein, were also clearly exemplified. Leveraging this account, we employed a set of mixed, complementary analytical methods to quantitatively analyze a set of open-ended survey responses indexing the user interfaces (i.e., pages/sections) that a sample of young adults reported commonly using over six popular platforms (i.e., TikTok, Instagram, Snapchat, YouTube, Facebook, and Twitter) as group-level representations. sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: Lastly, our results provide some insight into the structure of digital user interfaces within the Digital User Interface Model. First, as a user's notion of interfaces as discrete spaces can exist at multiple levels of abstraction (see Figure 1), they can vary in their level of detail and scope across a platform as a bounded application (see Figure 1, Box C). Although alternative frameworks implementing interfaces as focal constructs outline interfaces as digital spaces (e.g., Carter et al., 2023), they do not explicate interfaces to this degree of specificity. While it is not possible with the present data to identify the scope of each digital user interface described (i.e., how participant descriptions objectively map back onto an application), semantic labels (i.e., interface constructs) provided by participants theoretically spanned the gamut from individual content structures (e.g., post) to pages/tabs (e.g., Home on Instagram) to content feeds (e.g., Stories on Instagram). Thus, akin to the PSMEF's conceptualization of PMSEs in terms of a set of hierarchical, technological constructs (Carter et al., 2023), the Digital User Interface Model extended this notion of construct abstraction to digital user interfaces specifically based on a user's conception of platforms as a constellation of navigable, digital spaces. It further outlined the importance of modeling interfaces as places using group-level analytical techniques to account for individual-level discrepancies in how a place is understood/accounted across participants. To our knowledge, our analysis provides the first application of this using two methodological approaches.

In addition to facilitating thinking around digital user interfaces as a psychologically defined construct, the Digital User Interface Model also outlines why conceptualizing social media at the level of branded platforms is a higher-level, technological abstraction than individual interfaces (see Figure 1, Box B). Particularly, in such instances the platform as a whole is considered the digital space intermediating interaction; it subsumes the lower-order interface constructs existing within a platform. This conceptualization of an interface coincides with measuring social media in terms of branded platforms (e.g., passive Instagram use)-a common approach in social media effects research (Meier & Reinecke, 2021).

In advancing ways to quantitatively and theoretically organize qualitatively unique types of interfaces into different UICs, the Digital User Interface Model opens new inlets to advance the study of social media effects over time by providing a basis to continually organize mediated contexts based on their shared situational properties in line with the PSMEF (Carter et al., 2023). As platform brands compete, they adopt similar interface designs. This suggests that while platforms maintain idiosyncratic features that change over time, there will always be a tendency for UICs to emerge and provide sufficient levels of platform competition. As new platforms grow in popularity (e.g., TikTok), based on some design innovation (e.g., For You Page), competing platforms inevitably maintain an incentive to implement their own variation of the design space (e.g., Reels, Shorts, and Spotlight).

sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: Despite its strengths, this study has limitations. First, the present methodology partially relied on thematic coding. Nevertheless, the primary analysis used two forms of text analysis (i.e., SSNA and LDA) to quantitatively classify UICs inductively. Further, in contrast to previous work which coded for the presence/absence of interface concepts across text descriptions (i.e., Carter et al., 2023), this study thematically mapped reported pages/sections to navigable locations over each app indexed by a navigational icon in the app's primary navigational menus. Mapping reported sections/pages to prominent navigational icons provided a unitary basis for the comparison of interfaces across different platforms-specifically, given platforms' hierarchical structure (e.g., Newsfeed is embedded in the Home Tab on Facebook) and their role as primary in-app navigational components (Shneiderman et al., 2017). However, the resulting pattern of associations may differ when mapped onto other technological structures (e.g., content streams versus primary tabs), as they also function as types of UICs (e.g., Stories and Posts).

Lastly, given the low level of reporting for particular platforms and certain types of interfaces, we did not have sufficient data to assess the unique characteristics of all interfaces that participants reported using to evidence prospective types of UICs. Initial results suggest that language models are particularly apt in modeling commonly used UIC groupings. This likely stemmed from our prompt (e.g., asking about interfaces typically used); alternative lines of questioning may yield different UIC groupings. However, it is important to note that participants' understanding of interfaces likely depends on their familiarity with interfaces themselves (Uddin & Gutwin, 2021). Results imply the possible existence of alternative groupings of interfaces, including Notification Pages (e.g., Notifications on Twitter and Activity on Instagram), Digital Marketplaces (e.g., Shop on Instagram and Marketplace on Facebook), and Creation Pages (e.g., Create on Instagram and Record on TikTok). Additional work is needed to model users' PSMEs at scale to further validate proposed UICs and their corresponding attributes. Nevertheless, this study provides initial support for the potential of leveraging inductive modeling techniques as a means of evidencing UICs as core social media elements (Carter et al., 2023). Future work needs to explore ways of extending methods to less used interfaces, prioritizing how UICs differ by objective environmental cues: temporal context (e.g., recent and random), user segments, and behaviors (see Rauthmann & Sherman, 2020).

Despite its strengths, this study has limitations. First, the present methodology partially relied on thematic coding. Nevertheless, the primary analysis used two forms of text analysis (i.e., SSNA and LDA) to quantitatively classify UICs inductively. Further, in contrast to previous work which coded for the presence/absence of interface concepts across text descriptions (i.e., Carter et al., 2023), this study thematically mapped reported pages/sections to navigable locations over each app indexed by a navigational icon in the app's primary navigational menus. Mapping reported sections/pages to prominent navigational icons provided a unitary basis for the comparison of interfaces across different platforms-specifically, given platforms' hierarchical structure (e.g., Newsfeed is embedded in the Home Tab on Facebook) and their role as primary in-app navigational components (Shneiderman et al., 2017). sections.paper_id: Carter et al. - 2023 - A replication and extension of the Personal Social Media Ecosystem Framework
paragraphs: Social media has evolved into an increasingly pluralistic, multiplatform reality. Today's social media landscape will likely look very different from tomorrow's, however. We hope that the methodological and theoretical contributions of this work will help researchers further organize social media effects research and keep pace with the continual updates seen across the commercial social media landscape-particularly, by mapping correlates of social media use onto UICs, among other social media elements.
